title: Variables & Mutability 🔒
description: Learn why variables in Rust are immutable by default.

slides.title: Declaring Variables 📝
slides.content:
In Rust, we use the `let` keyword to create variables.

```rust
let apples = 5;
```

Pretty simple, right?

slides.title: The Catch 🎣
slides.content:
What happens if we try to change the number of apples?

```rust
let apples = 5;
apples = 10; // ERROR! 🚨
```

Unlike most languages, variables in Rust are **immutable** by default. Once a value is bound to a variable, it is locked in forever.

quizData.question: What happens if you try to change the value of a regular `let` variable?

options.text: The compiler throws an error because variables are immutable by default.

quizData.explanation: Rust enforces immutability by default at compile time to ensure safety.

slides.title: Making Things Change 🔄
slides.content:
# Enter `mut`

If you *want* a variable to change, you have to explicitly tell Rust using the `mut` (mutable) keyword.

```rust
let mut apples = 5;
apples = 10; // Works perfectly! 🎉
```

quizData.question: Which keyword allows a variable to be reassigned later?
quizData.explanation: Adding `mut` after `let` makes the variable mutable (changeable).

slides.title: Why Immutable? 🛡️
slides.content:
Why does Rust make you type extra words just to change a variable?

**Safety and Concurrency!** When variables are immutable by default, Rust guarantees that data won't unexpectedly change out from under you. This makes tracking down bugs infinitely easier.
